purpose: Draft (inactive) question objects derived from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-4 batch -- a completed, handwritten 'Review Quiz #4' (Water Survival, SHLSOS Course #549), 24 fill-in-the-blank questions across 6 pages. These are NOT part of src/content/course.json's live "questions" array and will not appear in the app until explicitly promoted.
activationRule: Do not add any of these questions to course.json unless its verificationStatus has been changed away from 'draft-unverified'. q-quiz4-safetyfactor-launchinggear is recommended needs-review, not quiz-derived-study -- see its sourceReference for why.
duplicateCheckNotes: Cross-referenced against all 235 live questions in src/content/course.json before drafting -- see the fuller duplicateCheckNotes in vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json for specifics. Dropped as duplicates: Q21 (Rottmer timing, duplicates q-ch2-rottmer-hard), Q22 (deviation, duplicates q-ch2-compass3way-vh), Q7 (tricing pendant function, too close to q-gloss-tricing), Q11 (sea painter secured forward, duplicates q-ch2-seapainteruse). Q13 was left blank by the student. Q24's answer directly CONTRADICTS the already-live q-quiz1a-coi-lifeboatmen-source (this quiz says SOLAS/Station Bill; that question says Certificate of Inspection) -- not drafted, flagged for Vinci to resolve. Two internal near-duplicate pairs within this quiz were merged into single questions: Q1+Q18 (limit switch, davit type + function) and Q14+Q19 (gravity davit, most-common + one-person-operated).

questions.prompt: On a roller gravity davit, what does the limit switch do?

choices.text: Prevents the lifeboat from being raised all the way up to the davit stops
Automatically starts the engine once the boat is waterborne
choices.misconception: Confuses the limit switch with an unrelated engine-starting function.

choices.text: Sounds an alarm if the boat is lowered too slowly
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated speed-alarm function -- the limit switch is about over-travel, not speed.

choices.text: Releases the gripes automatically at the start of launching
choices.misconception: Confuses the limit switch with the separate gripes/cradle-securing system.

hints: Think about what stops the boat from being cranked or winched too far in the wrong direction, back up into the davit structure itself.
This fitting is specifically associated with roller gravity davits.
questions.explanation: A limit switch, found on roller gravity davits, prevents the lifeboat from being raised all the way up to the davit stops -- guarding against over-travel damage during recovery.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, images 2026-07-07_064994-1.jpg and 2026-07-07_064994-5.jpg (Review Quiz #4, questions 1 and 18 of 24, combined -- question 1 asked which davit type has a limit switch, question 18 asked what it does; merged into one question rather than drafted as two overlapping questions on the same fitting); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What purpose do a lifeboat's gripes serve?

choices.text: They fasten the lifeboat firmly against the ship's davit frame or cradle when it's in a stowed position
They release the boat cleanly from the falls once it's safely in the water
choices.misconception: Describes the separate hook/releasing-gear system, not the gripes' cradle-securing function.

choices.text: They regulate the lowering speed of a gravity davit
choices.misconception: Confuses gripes with the governor/centrifugal brake, a different fitting entirely.

choices.text: They provide handholds for crew boarding the boat
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated boarding-aid function.

hints: Think about what keeps the boat from shifting in its cradle while the ship is underway, before any launch begins.
questions.explanation: Lifeboat gripes fasten the boat firmly against the ship's davit frame or cradle while it's in its stowed position -- a separate function from the hook/releasing-gear system that lets the boat go once it's in the water, or the turnbuckle that removes slack from the gripes themselves.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-1.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 2 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What mechanism is used to secure an open lifeboat's sea painter to the forward inboard thwart?

choices.text: A toggle pin arrangement
A permanent weld
choices.misconception: A permanently welded painter couldn't be released or replaced -- the attachment must be a releasable fitting.

choices.text: Ordinary knotted rope, with no hardware
choices.misconception: Understates the attachment -- a specific pin fitting is used, not just a knot.

choices.text: The same turnbuckle used to tension the gripes
choices.misconception: Confuses the sea painter's attachment hardware with the separate turnbuckle used on the gripes.

hints: This is a quick-release-style pin fitting, distinct from a permanent or improvised attachment.
questions.explanation: An open lifeboat's sea painter is secured to a strong point on the forward inboard thwart using a toggle pin arrangement.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-1.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 3 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json. Note: the live q-ch2-seapainteruse already tests WHERE the sea painter is secured (the forward one-third of the boat); this question deliberately tests only the toggle-pin attachment mechanism, a distinct new detail.

questions.prompt: What prevents a lifeboat fall's block rings from prematurely falling out of the releasing hooks?

choices.text: A locking cam behind the main hook, plus a hydrostatic interlock
A second, backup set of falls
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated redundant-rigging solution -- the actual answer is a specific locking/interlock mechanism on the hook itself.

choices.text: Nothing -- the block rings are held in place only by tension on the falls
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, deliberate safety mechanism -- tension alone isn't the safeguard.

choices.text: The gripes
choices.misconception: Confuses this with the separate gripes system that secures the boat in its stowed cradle, not the hook/block-ring interface.

hints: Two separate safeguards work together here: one purely mechanical, one that only allows release once the boat is in the water.
questions.explanation: A locking cam behind the main hook, combined with a hydrostatic interlock, prevents a lifeboat fall's block rings from prematurely falling out of the releasing hooks.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-1.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 4 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: If a ship is pitching and rolling excessively, how should a davit's frapping lines be rigged?

choices.text: Fore and aft
Athwartship (side to side)
choices.misconception: The wrong orientation for controlling excessive pitching and rolling motion.

choices.text: Straight down, vertically
choices.misconception: Doesn't provide the fore-and-aft control needed in a pitching, rolling sea.

choices.text: Frapping lines aren't used in rough weather
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual guidance -- frapping lines matter most, and need a specific rigging orientation, precisely when conditions are rough.

hints: Think about which direction of boat motion (pitching/rolling) needs the most control as the boat is steadied against the ship's side.
questions.explanation: When a ship is pitching and rolling excessively, a davit's frapping lines should be rigged fore and aft, to help steady the lifeboat against the ship's side under those conditions.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-2.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 5 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json. Note: the live q-gloss-frapping already tests the general definition/purpose of frapping lines; this question tests the specific fore-and-aft rigging detail for rough-weather conditions, which that question doesn't cover.

questions.prompt: What is the general sequence of steps for lowering an open lifeboat (classroom model)?

choices.text: Preparing the boat, swinging it out over the ship's side, boarding, then safely releasing it into the water
Boarding, preparing the boat, swinging it out, then releasing it
choices.misconception: Boards before the boat is even prepared or swung out -- out of order.

choices.text: Swinging the boat out, releasing it into the water, then boarding once it's afloat
choices.misconception: Releases the boat into the water before anyone has boarded -- reverses the intended sequence.

choices.text: Preparing the boat, boarding immediately, then swinging it out and releasing it in one motion
choices.misconception: Skips swinging the boat out over the ship's side before boarding begins.

hints: Think about the boat needing to be ready and positioned outboard before anyone climbs in.
questions.explanation: The general sequence for lowering an open lifeboat is: preparing the boat, swinging it out over the ship's side, boarding, and then safely releasing it into the water.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-2.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 6 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What piece of equipment regulates the speed at which a gravity davit lowers a lifeboat, and what is the maximum speed?

choices.text: A governor / centrifugal brake, with a maximum speed of 120 feet per minute
The limit switch, with no maximum speed specified
choices.misconception: Confuses the limit switch (which prevents over-raising) with the separate speed-regulating governor/brake.

choices.text: A governor / centrifugal brake, with a maximum speed of 12 feet per minute
choices.misconception: Understates the actual maximum speed by a factor of 10.

choices.text: A hand crank, with the speed controlled entirely by the operator
choices.misconception: A hand crank is used for mechanical davits, not for regulating a gravity davit's automatic lowering speed.

hints: Think about a mechanical device that resists speed increases as it spins faster -- that's the basic principle of a centrifugal brake.
questions.explanation: A governor / centrifugal brake regulates the speed at which a gravity davit lowers a lifeboat, with a maximum speed of 120 feet per minute.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-3.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 8 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the best procedure for positioning a ship to pick up a lifeboat at sea using the lifeboat's sea painter?

choices.text: Position the ship ahead of and to leeward of the lifeboat, with the wind broad on the bow
Position the ship directly downwind of the lifeboat, letting the wind push the ship toward it
choices.misconception: Ignores the ahead-and-leeward positioning that actually makes pickup safe and controllable.

choices.text: Position the ship astern of the lifeboat and approach from directly behind
choices.misconception: Not the recommended relative position -- the ship should be ahead of the lifeboat, not astern.

choices.text: Position doesn't matter, as long as the sea painter is ready to pass
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, specific positioning procedure as irrelevant.

hints: Think about keeping the lifeboat in a controlled, predictable position relative to the ship's hull and the wind, rather than letting either drift unpredictably.
questions.explanation: To pick up a lifeboat at sea using its sea painter, the ship should be positioned ahead of and to leeward of the lifeboat, with the wind broad on the bow -- giving controlled, predictable positioning for the pickup.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-3.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 9 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What kinds of signals are used when conducting lifeboat launching or recovery operations?

choices.text: A combination of internationally standardized sound signals, visual communication, and verbal commands
Verbal commands only -- sound and visual signals are reserved for distress situations
choices.misconception: Understates the actual signal toolkit used during routine launching/recovery operations.

choices.text: Radio communication only, with no sound or visual signals
choices.misconception: Radio isn't the standard method described -- sound, visual, and verbal signals are all used together.

choices.text: There's no standardized approach -- each crew develops its own signals
choices.misconception: Dismisses the internationally standardized nature of these signals.

hints: Think about redundancy -- if one signal type can't be seen or heard clearly, another type should still get the message across.
questions.explanation: Lifeboat launching and recovery operations rely on a combination of internationally standardized sound signals, visual communication, and verbal commands, rather than any single method alone.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-3.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 10 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What must be done prior to using the hand crank on a lifeboat davit's winch?

choices.text: Disable the power / motor
Fully lower the lifeboat first
choices.misconception: Not the actual precaution -- the hand crank can be needed at various stages, not only after full lowering.

choices.text: Remove the fall wires entirely
choices.misconception: The fall wires stay in place -- removing them would defeat the winch's purpose.

choices.text: Nothing -- the hand crank can be used at any time, powered or not
choices.misconception: Ignores a real safety precaution -- using the hand crank while power is still engaged would be hazardous.

hints: Think about what would happen if the motor engaged while someone was manually cranking the same mechanism.
questions.explanation: Power/motor must be disabled prior to using a winch's hand crank -- a precaution against the motor engaging while someone is manually operating the same mechanism.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-4.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 12 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json. Note: the source question also asked WHY this matters, but the student's answer only addressed what must be done -- this question tests only the source-supported half.

questions.prompt: What properties describe the type of wire used to construct lifeboat fall wires?

choices.text: Rotation-resistant and corrosion-resistant steel wire rope
Flexible nylon-coated wire, chosen for its light weight
choices.misconception: Not the actual material -- fall wires are steel wire rope, not a nylon-coated alternative.

choices.text: Plain uncoated steel wire, with no special corrosion treatment
choices.misconception: Understates the actual specification -- corrosion resistance is a deliberate requirement, not absent.

choices.text: Galvanized chain, not wire rope at all
choices.misconception: Confuses fall wires with a chain-based alternative -- falls are wire rope.

hints: Think about two separate stresses a fall wire is under: constant bending/twisting as it runs over sheaves and drums, and ongoing exposure to a marine environment.
questions.explanation: Lifeboat fall wires are rotation-resistant and corrosion-resistant steel wire rope.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-4.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 15 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json. Note: the already-live q-quiz3-winch-falls-material tests the specific wire-rope CONSTRUCTION used for winch-davit falls (6x19 regular lay, filler wire); this question tests the more general rotation-resistant/corrosion-resistant PROPERTIES of lifeboat fall wire generally, a related but distinct angle -- flagged given the topical closeness.

questions.prompt: What type of davit must be hand-cranked to move the lifeboat from its stowed position to the ship's side for lowering?

choices.text: Mechanical davits
Gravity davits
choices.misconception: Gravity davits move the boat to the ship's side using gravity, not hand-cranking.

choices.text: Roller davits
choices.misconception: Not the davit type specifically associated with hand-cranking to reach the ship's side.

choices.text: No davit type requires hand-cranking for this step
choices.misconception: Mechanical davits specifically require hand-cranking for this step.

hints: Think about the davit type that relies on manual effort rather than gravity to get the boat moving toward the ship's side.
questions.explanation: Mechanical davits must be hand-cranked to move the lifeboat from its stowed position to the ship's side for lowering -- unlike gravity davits, which use the boat's own weight for this movement.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-5.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 16 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What's the proper way to safely launch a lifeboat when the ship is making way (still moving)?

choices.text: Reduce the vessel's speed, to minimize the effects of propeller wash and drag
Increase the vessel's speed, so the lifeboat clears the hull faster
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual guidance -- more speed means more propeller wash and drag, not less.

choices.text: Speed makes no difference to a safe launch
choices.misconception: Dismisses a real, deliberate precaution -- vessel speed directly affects propeller wash and drag during launch.

choices.text: Turn the ship broadside to minimize the effects of the wind only
choices.misconception: Focuses on wind rather than the actual concern described -- propeller wash and drag from the ship's own movement.

hints: Think about what a moving hull and a turning propeller do to the water right where a lifeboat is being lowered.
questions.explanation: The proper way to safely launch a lifeboat when the ship is making way is to reduce the vessel's speed, minimizing the effects of propeller wash and drag on the lifeboat during launch.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-5.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 17 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What type of lifeboat davit is both the most common found on ships today and designed to be operated by one person?

choices.text: The gravity davit
The mechanical davit
choices.misconception: Mechanical davits require hand-cranking, not the simpler one-person operation described here.

choices.text: The roller davit
choices.misconception: Not the davit type this specific combination of facts describes.

choices.text: No single davit type is designed for one-person operation
choices.misconception: The gravity davit specifically is designed for exactly this.

hints: Think about the davit type that uses the lifeboat's own weight to do most of the work, requiring less manual effort from the operator.
questions.explanation: The gravity davit is both the most common and easily operated lifeboat davit type found on ships today, and it's specifically designed to be operated by one person.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, images 2026-07-07_064994-4.jpg and 2026-07-07_064994-5.jpg (Review Quiz #4, questions 14 and 19 of 24, combined -- both ask about the same davit type from complementary angles; merged into one question rather than drafted as two overlapping questions); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: What is the "Minimum Safety Factor" required for all lifeboat launching gear?

choices.text: 3 (i.e. gear must be rated to withstand at least 3 times its expected working load)
1 (i.e. gear only needs to withstand exactly its expected working load, with no margin)
choices.misconception: A safety factor of exactly 1 provides no margin at all, which defeats the purpose of a safety factor.
Overstates the required minimum safety factor.

choices.text: There is no minimum safety factor specified for lifeboat launching gear
choices.misconception: A specific minimum safety factor is required, not left unspecified.

hints: Think of this as a multiple of the gear's expected working load, not a distance or a percentage.
questions.explanation: The Minimum Safety Factor for all lifeboat launching gear is 3 -- meaning the gear must be rated to withstand at least 3 times its expected working load.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-5.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 20 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json. CONFIDENCE NOTE: the student's handwritten answer reads '3M', which is ambiguous notation -- 'Minimum Safety Factor' figures in this domain are conventionally plain ratios (e.g. a multiple of working load), and 'M' isn't a standard unit for this kind of factor, so this was interpreted as '3' (with 'M' most likely shorthand for 'Minimum,' echoing the question's own wording) rather than treated as part of the numeric value. This interpretation was NOT directly confirmed and should be double-checked against the manual or with Vinci before being treated as fully settled. Recommended needs-review rather than quiz-derived-study for this reason.

questions.prompt: What should be done with a lifeboat's magnetic compass when it's not in use?

choices.text: Removed from its mounting bracket and securely stowed in its designated storage box inside the lifeboat
Left mounted in place at all times, whether in use or not
choices.misconception: Leaves the compass mounted and exposed unnecessarily when the boat isn't in use.

choices.text: Left mounted, but covered with a waterproof bag
choices.misconception: Understates the actual requirement -- the compass must be removed and stowed, not just covered in place.

choices.text: Given to the coxswain to keep in a personal locker
choices.misconception: Invents an informal storage arrangement rather than the designated storage box inside the lifeboat itself.

hints: Think about protecting the compass from damage and keeping it out of the way when the boat isn't actively in use.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat's magnetic compass, when not in use, should be removed from its mounting bracket and securely stowed in its designated storage box inside the lifeboat.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-4, image 2026-07-07_064994-6.jpg (Review Quiz #4, question 23 of 24); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-4-source-intake.json
